## Description

[![@simonschmeisser](https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/372442?v=4&size=48)](https://github.com/simonschmeisser)

[simonschmeisser](https://github.com/simonschmeisser)

opened [on Mar 18, 2020on Mar 18, 2020](https://github.com/moveit/moveit/issues/1958#issue-583541688)

Issue body actions

We're currently discussing internally how to integrate external safety features with MoveIt. What we aim for is a very basic integration of area sensors that report something along the lines of 100% 50% 10% 0% depending on proximity of humans. The sensor is of course directly coupled (PROFIsafe or similar) to the hardware and MoveIt/ROS is just an observer.

This could be handled by having `TrajectoryExecutionManager` listen to a new topic with such information. I'm wondering if this has been done previously and if there are message types defined somewhere already?

The message would include

- int safety\_state
- double resulting velocity override

[@gavanderhoorn](https://github.com/gavanderhoorn) do you know of previous efforts? Should this be included in `industrial_msgs::RobotStatus`? (I personally would rather have it separately)

## Activity

[![](https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/372442?s=64&v=4)simonschmeisser](https://github.com/simonschmeisser)

added

[enhancement](https://github.com/moveit/moveit/issues?q=state%3Aopen%20label%3A%22enhancement%22)

[on Mar 18, 2020on Mar 18, 2020](https://github.com/moveit/moveit/issues/1958#event-3140349716)

[![v4hn](https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/680358?u=a791eace2598e8a679b710ee9eed5614e77fe475&v=4&size=80)](https://github.com/v4hn)

### v4hn commented on Mar 18, 2020on Mar 18, 2020

[![@v4hn](https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/680358?u=a791eace2598e8a679b710ee9eed5614e77fe475&v=4&size=48)](https://github.com/v4hn)

[v4hn](https://github.com/v4hn)

[on Mar 18, 2020on Mar 18, 2020](https://github.com/moveit/moveit/issues/1958#issuecomment-600555291) via email

Contributor

More actions

What features would you like to retain in MoveIt if the hardware driver takes care of the slowdown?

Essentially, if you disable duration monitoring, there is no need to modify MoveIt at all.
Trajectories will simply take longer than anticipated.

If you want to have online path validation, you would have to extend the monitor.

The two options I see:

\- you can monitor the safety state and update the expected runtime of the remaining trajectory.
You are probably already aware of [https://github.com/ros-industrial/industrial\_core/tree/kinetic-devel/industrial\_msgs](https://github.com/ros-industrial/industrial_core/tree/kinetic-devel/industrial_msgs)
These messages do not seem to contain information like that yet, but I would expect it there.
This approach will end up much more fragile than the current time-based solution because delays can accumulate
and you might have to model the dynamic response of the controller to changes in safety state if you want to stay close to the real trajectory.

\- You can directly monitor joint states and map them to the executing trajectory by hand.
This also allows to verify execution tolerances.

Personally, I would like to see an upstream patch for the second option. 😎

[![simonschmeisser](https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/372442?v=4&size=80)](https://github.com/simonschmeisser)

### simonschmeisser commented on Mar 18, 2020on Mar 18, 2020

[![@simonschmeisser](https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/372442?v=4&size=48)](https://github.com/simonschmeisser)

[simonschmeisser](https://github.com/simonschmeisser)

[on Mar 18, 2020on Mar 18, 2020](https://github.com/moveit/moveit/issues/1958#issuecomment-600560150)

ContributorAuthor

More actions

> - You can directly monitor joint states and map them to the executing trajectory by hand. This also allows to verify execution tolerances.

Thanks for your feedback [@v4hn](https://github.com/v4hn) , can you elaborate a bit more?

Turning off the duration monitoring would unfortunately mean that our application "hangs" as there are multiple components without a feedback channel. (E.g. upload to robot failed, robot rejected trajectory, ...) We should modify our application such that one can still cancel such trajectories but we also need to notify higher level system or maintenance personal in case of errors.

[![v4hn](https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/680358?u=a791eace2598e8a679b710ee9eed5614e77fe475&v=4&size=80)](https://github.com/v4hn)

### v4hn commented on Mar 18, 2020on Mar 18, 2020

[![@v4hn](https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/680358?u=a791eace2598e8a679b710ee9eed5614e77fe475&v=4&size=48)](https://github.com/v4hn)

[v4hn](https://github.com/v4hn)

[on Mar 18, 2020on Mar 18, 2020](https://github.com/moveit/moveit/issues/1958#issuecomment-600575269)

Contributor

More actions

> > You can directly monitor joint states and map them to the executing trajectory by hand. This also allows to verify execution tolerances.
>
> can you elaborate a bit more?

You send trajectories to the controller and expect it to follow that trajectory in joint-space, though the time profile can change from the computed one.

For this use case, you can monitor the `joint_state` topic and try to infer the next upcoming waypoint from the current state. This would enable a much more reliable estimate of the current state of execution, in terms of progress through the trajectory.

The current default estimation is entirely based on the time since execution started.

> Turning off the duration monitoring would unfortunately mean that our application "hangs" as there are multiple components without a feedback channel.

Does that mean all these components currently only rely on the time since you sent the trajectory for execution? I really would not build on that value at all...

> (E.g. upload to robot failed, robot rejected trajectory, ...)

These things should be reported by the `TrajectoryExecutionAction` itself. This feedback is independent of duration monitoring.

> We should modify our application such that one can still cancel such trajectories

If "such" refers to cases of action failure: this should already be the case?

If "such" refers to trajectories with very long execution times due to the safety states:

It can make a lot of sense to provide information on safety states via a topic and have higher-level logic subscribe to that topic if a change of safety state can change the system behavior and not just its time scaling.

If you want to reason about the remaining execution time in MoveIt though, I would monitor joint states, as discussed above, and introduce triggers if external time limits are exceeded.

[![simonschmeisser](https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/372442?v=4&size=80)](https://github.com/simonschmeisser)

### simonschmeisser commented on Mar 18, 2020on Mar 18, 2020

[![@simonschmeisser](https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/372442?v=4&size=48)](https://github.com/simonschmeisser)

[simonschmeisser](https://github.com/simonschmeisser)

[on Mar 18, 2020on Mar 18, 2020](https://github.com/moveit/moveit/issues/1958#issuecomment-600590780)

ContributorAuthor

More actions

See [ros-industrial/industrial\_core#187](https://github.com/ros-industrial/industrial_core/issues/187) and [ros-industrial/industrial\_core#131](https://github.com/ros-industrial/industrial_core/issues/131) and a few others for examples where the action is not cancelled cleanly

[![v4hn](https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/680358?u=a791eace2598e8a679b710ee9eed5614e77fe475&v=4&size=80)](https://github.com/v4hn)

### v4hn commented on Mar 18, 2020on Mar 18, 2020

[![@v4hn](https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/680358?u=a791eace2598e8a679b710ee9eed5614e77fe475&v=4&size=48)](https://github.com/v4hn)

[v4hn](https://github.com/v4hn)

[on Mar 18, 2020on Mar 18, 2020](https://github.com/moveit/moveit/issues/1958#issuecomment-600635513)

Contributor

More actions

You are pointing to a number of actual issues in driver implementations that should really be fixed! They are really old too, wow...

Of course you can build a monitoring system around the whole system to validate its behavior, but that's (theoretically) not the idea of the functional frameworks.

Monitoring shouldn't affect behavior during normal operation.

If I understand your intention, you are basically looking for a way to extend the monitoring system you already have (duration-based) to support safety states and dynamic external velocity scaling.

I would still argue that this dynamic component justifies joint state monitoring.

It also means you can define better criteria for monitoring alerts/failures, like

- some epsilon after sending the trajectory, the joints should start moving and have velocities going in the correct direction
- if the next way point is not passed within a maximum time, defined as either some (rather high) constant times the expected duration for the trajectory segment, report stuck


Alternatively, the same check could use the reported safety state factor and a smaller constant `>1` to account for delays and dynamic effects
- if the last way point is not reached up to some accuracy at the end, report failure to reach goal

# The open source motion planning platform for [ROS](https://picknik.ai/ros/)

[Watch overview](https://www.youtube.com/embed/_wazsgxZiiM?rel=0&showinfo=0&autoplay=1) [Comparison of MoveIt 2 vs MoveIt Pro](https://picknik.ai/moveit-2-vs-moveit-pro/)

## What is MoveIt?

MoveIt is an open source robotics manipulation framework for developing new applications, prototyping designs, and benchmarking algorithms.

Are you a commercial company looking to quickly deploy robots to production? Check out [MoveIt Pro](https://picknik.ai/pro).


![double quotes icon](https://picknik.ai/assets/images/quotes.png)

#### MoveIt saved us approximately 6 months of development time and allowed us to get our products in front of customers well ahead of schedule.

Tom Hummel, VP Engineering, Kindred

### Why MoveIt?

1.

#### No license fees

Open source, business friendly, and non-copyleft usage


2.

#### Extensible

Use only the components you need to bootstrap your application


3.

#### Fully customizable

You have full access to the source code so your team can modify


[Visit moveit's website](https://moveit.ai/)

### Key MoveIt Components

![motion planning icon](https://picknik.ai/assets/images/redesign/moveit-icons/ic-motion.svg)

Motion Planning

![advanced inverse kinematics icon](https://picknik.ai/assets/images/redesign/moveit-icons/ic-manipulation.svg)

Manipulation

![real time control icon](https://picknik.ai/assets/images/redesign/moveit-icons/ic-kinematics.svg)

Inverse Kinematics

![motion planning icon](https://picknik.ai/assets/images/icon3.png)

Realtime Control

![motion planning icon](https://picknik.ai/assets/images/redesign/moveit-icons/ic-3d.svg)

3D Perception

![motion planning icon](https://picknik.ai/assets/images/redesign/moveit-icons/ic-navigation.svg)

Collision Checking

![double quotes icon](https://picknik.ai/assets/images/quotes.png)

### MoveIt is making advanced motion planning mainstream. It can easily reduce your time-to-market by years

Michael Ferguson, Entrepreneur and ROS™ Consultant

### Three Primary Goals of MoveIt

![Industrial icon](https://picknik.ai/assets/images/industrial.svg)

#### Prototyping

Build your minimum viable product with this open source solution


![Research icon](https://picknik.ai/assets/images/research.svg)

#### Research

Advance international manipulation research by providing an open source infrastructure. No more reinventing the wheel.


![Benchmarking icon](https://picknik.ai/assets/images/benchmarking.svg)

#### Benchmarking

Compare the performance of algorithms, particularly planning algorithms, by providing a common platform for testing.


[Download the journal publication on moveit](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1404.3785.pdf)

## PickNik's Stewardship of MoveIt

PickNik was founded with the mission of providing powerful platforms for the robotics industry. MoveIt OSS is our original product, though notably MoveIt was originally conceived at Willow Garage. PickNik has since developed a professional, more mature version of MoveIt called [MoveIt Pro](https://picknik.ai/pro).

While PickNik leads the growth and maintenance of MoveIt, we believe it's important to acknowledge the vibrant **international community of collaborators and maintainers** that play a huge role by improving the software and adding new features.


![hero image](https://picknik.ai/assets/images/hero2.png)

##### Business Model

PickNik is a for-profit company that balances the need to improve the open source code of ROS and MoveIt with the need to develop commercial software - [MoveIt Pro](https://picknik.ai/pro).


##### Stewardship

**What PickNik does to improve the MoveIt project:**

- Provides engineers that review pull requests, fix regressions, and add new features to the open source.

- Provides organizational leadership, outreach, and publicity.

- Organizes many events throughout the year, including World MoveIt Day and MoveItCon.

- Mentors interns and Google Summer of Code students to improve the code.

- Runs monthly and weekly video call working groups for MoveIt developers.

- Contributes portions of code from commercial projects back to improving MoveIt and [ROS](https://picknik.ai/ros/).

- Makes binary/debian releases at intervals throughout the year.


##### Our Open Source Commitment

**We promise that:**

- We won't remove features from the open source codebase in order to make the same feature paid (features might be removed from the open source codebase due to changes in the application).

- We will always release and open source all tests that we have for an open source feature.

- The open source codebase will not contain any artificial limits (performance, etc.).

- We will always allow you to benchmark the performance of MoveIt.


### Looking for a commercially   supported version of MoveIt?

PickNik's [MoveIt Pro platform](https://picknik.ai/pro) is an easier to use, production-grade version of MoveIt that is ready to support your team's development efforts.


[Learn more about MoveIt Pro](https://picknik.ai/pro/)

### ERDC TR-23-23 ii

# Abstract

MOVEit, a widely used Robot Operating System framework, plans composite tasks, where the high-level sequence of actions is fixed and

## known in advance. However, these tasks need to be tailored and adapted

## to the environmental context. This framework uses custom trajectory

## planners, known as controllers, to solve goals that are fully defined within

## the configuration space. Libraries, such as the Open Motion Planning

## Library, provide a collection of motion planners that can solve task-space

goals. An exact spatial and joint replication of the robotic manipulator’s mechanics, typically Universal Robot Description Format and Semantic

Robot Description Format files, is required. Common arms such as the Panda-Manipulator and OpenMANIPULATOR-X provide these files in

their respective public repositories, but custom arms require significant modification or even a complete rewrite of these files.

#### ERDC TR-23-23

# 1 Introduction

## 1.1 Background

### MOVEit (Coleman et al. 2014) is a widely used Robot Operating System

### (ROS) framework that describes and plans composite tasks using a

### sequence of actions at a high level and individual joint and kinematic

### control at a low level (Gorner et al. 2019). The environmental context of

### these plans is defined in the realm of dynamic planning scenes. This

### means that it is possible to plan motions in either the Cartesian space or

### the joint space. MOVEit uses custom trajectory planners, known as

### controllers, to solve goals. These goals are usually fully defined within the

configuration space. Libraries, such as the Open Motion Planning Library (OMPL), provide a collection of motion planners (Motion Planners

available in MOVEit!—Planners Benchmarking documentation) that can solve tasks in the space, assuming that a description of the robotic

manipulator is provided (Soetens et al. 2020). Specifically, the description contains both Unified Robot Description Format (URDF) (ROS 2020) and

Semantic Robot Description Format (SRDF) (ROS wiki 2019) files. Common arms such as Panda-Manipulator (ROBOTIS 2023) and

OpenMANIPULATOR-X (GmbH 2020) provide these files in their respective public repositories, but custom arms require these files to be

### rewritten to their specific geometry.

MOVEit provides a roscpp interface and a Python application programming interface (API) known as moveit\_commander. This report

### discusses both, but production code should be written using the roscpp

### interface. While being faster and having more functionality, the roscpp

### interface is able to control the robot more finely than the Python API can.

### MOVEit has recently integrated constrained motion control in the

### Cartesian space using the OMPL (Maeyer 2020; Kingston et al. 2018). This

### greatly improves the mapping potential of robotic manipulators using

### sensors such as depth cameras or lidars. Historically, the best way to

### maintain a position or orientation constraint was to create a waypoint

### plan with a very high number of points. However, this methodology did

### not actually constrain movement. For movements near singularities, the

### planner would typically navigate away from the point, not holding any of

### the desired constraints. Incorporating OMPL has resolved several of these issues.

* * *

### ERDC TR-23-23

# 1.2 Objectives

## The US Army Corps of Engineers (USACE) owns, maintains, and operates

## over 700 large-scale dams with a growing number exceeding their design

## life. Inspecting the outlet works of the earth dams is hazardous for human

inspectors. The USACE Engineer Research and Development Center (ERDC) is developing a solution that is capable of performing detailed

## autonomous inspections while an operator teleoperates a robot. To inspect

## the outlet works we plan on using a fifteen-foot robot arm equipped with a

## camera. The use of a robotic arm will reduce the risk to personnel and

allow a rapid assessment. This research supports the “Revolutionize and Accelerate Decision Making” research and development priority,

## which states,

## USACE leverages data science, artificial intelligence, machine learning, robotics, trade space analytics,

## geospatial engineering, and advanced models and simulations to revolutionize geospatially informed

## decision making by understanding the operational environment, predicting likely outcomes, decreasing

## uncertainties by 80%, and shaping faster decisions that minimize risk, reduce costs, and accelerate

## mission delivery by 5× for military, civil works, and disaster response operations.

# 1.3 Approach and Scope

## In this report, we leverage a repository provided by the manufacturer of

## the robotic arm, HDT Global. The xacro file, provided by HDT Global, was

## used by MOVEit for motion planning, inverse kinematics (IK), and

## forward kinematics. If the xacro file is formatted properly, then it can also

## be used for simulation. MOVEit compiles the xacro file into a URDF file at

## runtime, which is typically loaded to the robot description parameter. This

# 2.2 Utilizing Movement Constraints

Before the implementation of movement constraints in MOVEit, the motion plans were generated by the Covariant Hamiltonian Optimization

for Motion Planning (CHOMP) (Ratliff et al. 2009) and Stochastic Trajectory Optimization for Motion Planning (STOMP) (Williams, n.d.)

## planners. However, CHOMP and STOMP had difficulty accurately

controlling the orientation and position of the arm for the duration of the motion (motion planners available in MOVEit!—Planners Benchmarking

## documentation; Maeyer 2020). Specifically, if we wanted the end effector

## to hold a constant orientation as it moved, that is, parallel to the ground

## plane, the built-in waypoint planner resulted in a choppy motion that did

## not hold points to a relative pose. With the incorporation of movement

## constraints into OMPL, it is now possible to maintain a desired orientation

## or position constraints along the robotic arm. This is achieved by passing a

## lambda function to the OMPL motion planners. This lambda function is

## included in the optimization of the path produced. The movement

## constraints are not perfect since (most) arms do not have infinite degrees

## of freedom (DoF). Constraining a point of the robotic arm essentially

## reduces the DoF available for movement. Thus, a range of error must be

passed with the constraint parameters so that the generated motion plans are smoother and the robotic arm can avoid singularities.

## This report only discusses using the movement constraints from the

roscpp interface. In order to use the MOVEit movement constraints in roscpp code, perform the following:

## Verify that the ROS node is spinning (running). Verify that the moveit::planning\_interface::PlanningSceneInterface is

## initialized. Verify that the moveit::planning\_interface::MoveGroupInterface is

## initialized.

# 2.5 Known Issues

There are a few issues with the HDT repository and the hdt\_nav package that future programmers should be aware of:

## • The configuration that HDT provided of their move group does not

## interact well with subscribers. Any attempt to subscribe from a roscpp

## file results in a synchronization issue. This is true for both simulation

## and nonsimulation time. A solution for this has not yet been found.

## • The HDT arm has joint restrictions due to the gigabit Power over

## Ethnet cable internal to the entire length of the arm. This can effect

## motions to and away from the stow position in order to protect the

## cable. When controlling the HDT arm through Rviz, always verify

## that the motion plan presented by MOVEit does not collide with

_unmapped obstacles. If the map generated_ by SLAM is accurate, this should not be an issue.

## • MOVEit always uses doubles instead of floats. Never use floats when programming with MOVEit.

## • Motion constraints are not cleared after a motion plan has been

## planned and executed. You must manually clear them after every motion plan.

## • The Eigen3 library has some linking issues that affect MOVEit. If

## building with catkin does not work, when including the Eigen3 library, reinstalling MOVEit may help.

## • None of the launch files provided by HDT work, aside from the bringup

## files. This includes all gazebo files and most move group files. When

using gazebo, follow the instructions listed in section Section 2.1.2 and use the launch files from the hdt\_nav package.

## • Assuming that the robot starts in the stow position, it is unlikely that

## the robot will discover where the ceiling is using the end effector

## camera. Thus, it may be prudent to set an obstacle matching the ceiling

## height in the URDF file. This object may also be added to the MOVEit

## planning scene by determining the height of the room prior to launch time.

# 2.6 Future Work

## In this report, we use the information that MOVEit provides to determine

## the position of the scanning sensor. Since each joint position is known, the

## pose of each link, from the kinematic chain, can be determined with high

## precision. Therefore, the pose of the end effector, relative to some fixed

## position near the base of the robot arm, is known within some margin of

## error. Vibration, sag, and gear slock are the main concerns. This

## information can used by SLAM, via a transform tree, to construct a real-

## time map of the environment using the robot arm. We have demonstrated

this in simulation with a Panda-Manipulator and physically on a custom OpenMANIPULATOR-X arm, leveraging Real-Time Appearance-Based

Mapping (Labbé and Michaud 2019) to perform SLAM. We were successful mapping the environment with the smaller

## OpenMANIPULATORX robot arm; we are curious to see how the

## aforementioned vibration, sag, and gear slock affect the longer HDT arm.

## To date, we have explored adding a camera to the virtual HDT arm, shown

## in Figure 5, and plan to do the same with the physical arm.

### ERDC TR-23-23

# 3 Conclusion

## It is clear that advances in the OMPL and MOVEit aid in mapping. Path

## constraints, although limited to orientation, position, and visibility, can be

## used to generate trajectories that can quickly map civil work structures.

## Furthermore, the HDT robotic arm repository can be used out of the box

## in real life. With modifications, the repository can also be used in simulation.

## In the future, providing a framework for arbitrary constraints (represented

## by a lambda function) will be the next step for SLAM using robotic arms.

Although the constraints provided in MOVEit are promising, they are also very limited. Using solely OMPL and movement constraints make

mapping nonplanar structures difficult. More work will be necessary to provide variable and complex movement constraints.

This is the ROS Kinetic version of the tutorials, intended for ROS Kinetic users only.

If you are using a ROS Melodic release, we recommmend the [Melodic tutorials](http://docs.ros.org/melodic/api/moveit_tutorials/html/index.html) or the latest [master branch tutorials](https://ros-planning.github.io/moveit_tutorials/).

* * *

# MoveIt Tutorials [¶](http://docs.ros.org/en/kinetic/api/moveit_tutorials/html/index.html\#moveit-tutorials "Permalink to this headline")

These tutorials will step you through using MoveIt with your robot. It is assumed the robot you are working with has already been configured to work with MoveIt - check the [list of robots running MoveIt](http://moveit.ros.org/robots/) to see whether MoveIt is already available for your robot. Otherwise, skip to the “Integration with a New Robot” tutorials, below. If you just want to quickly try MoveIt, use the default Panda robot as your quick-start demo.

To follow along with these tutorials you will need a **ROBOT\_moveit\_config** package. This tutorial will use the Panda robot from Franka Emika. To get a working **panda\_moveit\_config** package you can install from source, create your own, or install from Debian. We will walk through each method in the following Getting Started tutorial:

## Getting Started with MoveIt and RViz [¶](http://docs.ros.org/en/kinetic/api/moveit_tutorials/html/index.html\#getting-started-with-moveit-and-rviz "Permalink to this headline")

## MoveGroup - ROS Wrappers in C++ and Python [¶](http://docs.ros.org/en/kinetic/api/moveit_tutorials/html/index.html\#movegroup-ros-wrappers-in-c-and-python "Permalink to this headline")

The simplest way to use MoveIt through scripting is using the `move_group_interface`. This interface is ideal for beginners and provides unified access to many of the features of MoveIt.

- [Move Group C++ Interface](http://docs.ros.org/en/kinetic/api/moveit_tutorials/html/doc/move_group_interface/move_group_interface_tutorial.html)

Building more complex applications with MoveIt often requires developers to dig into MoveIt’s C++ API. As an added plus, using the C++ API directly skips many of the ROS Service/Action layers resulting in significantly faster performance.

## Master’s Final Thesis

## Master’s Degree in Automatic Control and Robotics (MUAR)

# Assessment of an open-source software (MoveIt) for robotics

# teaching and its applications to the Master’s degree in Automatic

# Control and Robotics

### Autor: Lucas Fosco Tornielli Director: Cristina Lampón Diestre

### Call: July 2024

### Escola Tècnica Superior d’Enginyeria Industrial de Barcelona

* * *

2

* * *

# Resum

El present treball pretén fer una investigació sobre la planificació de rutes i la manipulació de robots utilitzant el programari MoveIt, per al posterior estudi de les seves aplicacions en el Màster de Control Automàtic i Robòtica.

Es comença amb una revisió històrica de la planificació de rutes, destacant l’evolució dels algorismes des de mètodes simples fins a sistemes complexos capaços d’operar en temps real i en entorns dinàmics. També es comenten temes bàsics de programació en ROS 2. Aquesta base teòrica pretén explicar el mínim i necessari per entendre les explicacions que es faran en el següent apartat sobre l'eina.

MoveIt es destaca com a una plataforma unificada que integra múltiples planificadors de moviment i sistemes de percepció, per adaptar-se als diferents nivells de coneixement dels usuaris. Precisament, el seu punt fort és que, gràcies a la seva interfície gràfica, no és necessari saber programar per poder visualitzar la planificació i execució d’un camí en un braç robot. Però també es poden dur a terme tasques més complicades, sent el major limitador els coneixements de programació de l’usuari.

Durant l’explicació es pretén anar pujant el nivell amb cada apartat, també per aconseguir tasques més complexes. Es comença amb la integració d’un robot, exemplificat amb el model UR3 de Universal Robots, i la seva posterior configuració en MoveIt. Es prossegueix amb l’execució de moviments punt a punt i l’evasió d’objectes de col·lisió en l’entorn de simulació, i s’acaba amb un exemple de “pick and place”.

Una vegada estudiada l’eina, es procedirà a l’explicació de les possibles integracions en les assignatures del Màster que més podrien beneficiar-se. També es donaran possibles millores a l’aplicació tenint en compte els seus usos.

Finalment, s'acaba l'estudi amb l'exposició de les conclusions a què s’ha arribat referent als objectius plantejats.

* * *

# Resumen

Is"presente trabajo pretende hacer una investigación sobre la planificación de rutas y la manipulación de robots utilizando el software MoveIt, para el posterior estudio de sus aplicaciones en el Máster de Control Automático i Robótica.

Se comienza con una revisión histórica de la planificación de rutas, destacando la evolución de los algoritmos desde métodos simples hasta sistemas complejos capaces de operar en tiempo real y en entornos dinámicos. También se comentan temas básicos de programación en ROS 2. Esta base teórica pretende explicar lo mínimo y necesario para entender las explicaciones que se harán en el siguiente apartado sobre la herramienta.

MoveIt se destaca como una plataforma unificada que integra múltiples planificadores de movimiento y sistemas de percepción, para adaptarse a los diferentes niveles de conocimiento de los usuarios. Precisamente, su punto fuerte es que, gracias a su interfaz gráfica, no es necesario saber programar para poder visualizar la planificación y ejecución de un plan en un brazo robot. Pero también se pueden llevar a cabo labores más complicadas, siendo el mayor limitador los conocimientos de programación del usuario.

Durante la explicación se pretende ir subiendo el nivel con cada apartado, también para conseguir tareas más complejas. Se empieza con la integración de un robot, ejemplificado con el modelo UR3 de Universal Robots, y su posterior configuración en MoveIt. Se prosigue con la ejecución de movimientos punto a punto y la evasión de objetos de colisión en el entorno de simulación, y se acaba con un ejemplo de “pick and place”.

Una vez estudiada la herramienta, se procederá a la explicación de las posibles integraciones en las asignaturas del Máster que más podrían beneficiarse. También se darán posibles mejoras a la aplicación teniendo en cuenta sus usos.

Finalmente, se termina el estudio con la exposición de las conclusiones a las que se ha llegado referente a los objetivos planteados.

* * *

# Abstract

The present work aims to conduct research on route planning and robot manipulation using the MoveIt software, for subsequent study of its applications in the Master's program in Automatic Control and Robotics..

It begins with a historical review of route planning, highlighting the evolution of algorithms from simple methods to complex systems capable of operating in real-time and dynamic environments. Basic programming topics in ROS 2 are also discussed. This theoretical foundation aims to explain the minimum necessary to understand the explanations that will be made in the next section about the tool.

MoveIt stands out as a unified platform that integrates multiple motion planners and perception systems, to accommodate different levels of user knowledge. Precisely, its strong point is that, thanks to its graphical interface, it is not necessary to know how to program in order to visualize the planning and execution of a path in a robotic arm. However, more complex tasks can also be carried out, with the main limitation being the user’s programming knowledge.

During the explanation, the intention is to gradually increase the level with each section, also to achieve more complex tasks. It starts with the integration of a robot, exemplified by the UR3 model from Universal Robots, and its subsequent configuration in MoveIt. It continues with the execution of point-to-point movements and the avoidance of collision objects in the simulation environment, ending with a pick and place example.

Once the tool has been studied, the possible integrations into the Master’s courses that could benefit the most will be explained. Possible improvements to the application will also be suggested, taking its uses into account.

Finally, the study concludes with the presentation of the conclusions reached regarding the objectives set.

* * *

# 1\. Introduction

Path planning, is a fundamental discipline in the field of robotics. It focuses on developing algorithms and methods to guide a robot from an initial point to a goal point, avoiding obstacles and optimizing specific criteria such as time, energy, or distance.

In the world of robotics, plan efficient and safely routes is crucial for execute complex tasks and ensuring safe interaction with humans and other robots. The evolution of path planning has enabled robots to operate in real-time and with dynamic environments, adapting and optimizing their behavior based on environmental conditions.

_**Figure 1. Example of path planning and obstacle avoidance in a dynamic**_

_environment. Source: \[1\]._

The development of applications that can simulate these processes allows professionals to test and refine algorithms in virtual environments before implementing them in physical robots. Additionally, they provide a platform for teaching and learning, facilitating the understanding of complex robotics and programming concepts without the need for access to real and expensive robots.

The objectives of this work are to investigate and analyze the specific capabilities of the MoveIt software for path planning and robot manipulation. During the learning process, the goal is to implement, configure, and control a UR3 robot. Starting with executing basic movements, the aim is to gradually achieve more advanced tasks in a simulation environment. The ultimate purpose is to explore potential integrations of MoveIt into the Master's courses, identifying those that could most benefit from its applications, and even proposing improvements and adaptations to the MoveIt software, considering its possible uses in educational and professional contexts.

Regarding the scope, the work will mainly focus on providing predominantly theoretical information about path and motion planning on the MoveIt platform, along with some more general aspects of robotics. It will not delve into more complex topics, such as the control of manipulators, and although many explanations will be accompanied by code, but not all the necessary code for all developments that will be presented.

To start understanding the concepts to be discussed, it is important to explain the current outlook of planners and the available software.

* * *

2. State of the art

2.1. Path planning software’s

The main objective of path planning is to develop a route from a starting point to the desired
destination, avoiding obstacles and optimizing certain criteria such as time, energy or
distance. Historically, path planning has evolved from simple algorithms for static
environments to complex systems capable of operating in dynamic and real-time
environments.

The relevance of path planning increased significantly with the Shakey mobile robot project
in the 1960s, “the first mobile robot with the ability to reason about its own actions” (Peter
Hart, 1972, \[2\]). From then on, visibility graph methods for navigation around obstacles
began to be invented Over the years, with advances in computing and robotics, path
planning techniques have evolved to address more complex and dynamic applications in
diverse environments, from manufacturing to autonomous exploration \[3\].

This continuous development has been driven by the need of robots and automated
systems to operate at ever-increasing speeds, without compromising accuracy and safety,
leading to the creation of sophisticated algorithms that optimize safe and efficient routes for
various robotic geometries and computational constraints.

There are a lot of methodologies, however, the classical motion planning methods are
usually separated into: roadmap techniques, cell decomposition and artificial potential
methods.

− Roadmap techniques: create an abstract "map" of the configuration space using
nodes and edges to represent possible paths. Nodes represent collision-free
configurations, and edges represent viable paths between them.

− Cell Decomposition methods: this involves dividing the configuration space into
regions or cells, so each cell can be easily navigated, and contiguous cells are
connected if it is possible to move between them without collisions.

* * *

Table 1. Principal libraries and softwares of path planning. Sources: \[4\], \[5\], \[6\], \[7\], \[8\], \[9\], \[10\].

When selecting a motion planning software, there are several aspects to consider.
Complexity is a key factor, as it determines how many degrees of freedom, constraints
and obstacles the robot can handle. It is also need to consider the speed and accuracy
required for the robot's motion, as well as its scalability for large-scale and/or multi-robot
problems. Finally, compatibility with hardware and software platforms should be
considered to facilitate integration into the desired environment.

In the case of robotic arms, path planning enables precise manipulation of objects,
essential in assembly lines and other non-industrial jobs such as robots used in surgical
operations \[11\]. They are also very useful for increasing safety in collaborative robot
environments, as one of the most common goals of path planning is to avoid collisions,
especially in dynamic environments.

On the other hand, mobile robots can use path planning to navigate unexplored terrain
and perform tasks without direct human intervention (e.g. in space exploration and
rescue tasks), which is vital in situations where the environment may be dangerous or
inaccessible to humans.

2.2. MoveIt

Knowing the importance of using the planner that best suits each case, a tool was
needed to unify the most important motion and path planners in a simulation platform
adaptable to different levels of knowledge, hence the idea of MoveIt.

MoveIt is the leading software library for motion planning and mobile manipulation in
ROS (Robotic Operating System), incorporating the latest advances in motion planning,
control and perception. It was created in 2011 by a group of researchers at Willow
Garage and has been updated since then thanks to a multitude of programmers who can
contribute with their innovations \[12\].

During its use, several features can be detected, for example:

# 3\. Theoretical background

To understand the utilities of MoveIt it is important to understand the importance of path planning, its historical evolution and the most important algorithms used. The large majority of these algorithms are integrated using ROS 2 (Robotic Operating System 2), which is a middleware widely used in robotics.

Thanks to ROS 2, several libraries can be integrated into a single system in order to simulate a real environment as closely as possible. For this reason, the following sections aim to provide a solid base of knowledge that facilitates the understanding of the more technical sections that will follow.

## 3.1. Path planning algorithms

As commented before, the evolution of path planning algorithms has evolved according to the needs of advances in automated robotic systems. From the beginning, it has been considered a priority to obtain a planner capable of finding a feasible path as quickly as possible, and with the computational limitations still present, the optimization of the algorithms has been one of the main objectives.

This search has led to develop a large number of path planning methods, each one adapted to specific needs and resources. In the following section, an overview of the most common algorithms of the so-called classical methods is given, including sampling- based planning algorithms, and ending with heuristic-based algorithms.

### 3.1.1. Classical methods: Cell decomposition

Classical algorithms can be differentiated in several ways. The most common is to separate between Cell Decomposition, Potential Field Methods and Road Maps. Cell decomposition-based methods are one out of three classical path planning families, which idea is to divide the workspace into manageable regions, known as cells.

The fundamental idea behind cell decomposition is to reduce the search space by dividing the environment into smaller regions, reducing the difficulty of the problem by quickly deleting unavailable areas for path computation. Each cell can be totally occupied by an obstacle, partially occupied or totally free, and only the last two types of cells are considered for path planning.

Three ways to do cell decomposition can be considered:

-Exact Cell Decomposition: the space is divided into simple polygonal cells that together form an exact coverage of the free space. The path is then constructed by joining the centres of the free polygons. The result, even being correct, can have unnatural or not-smooth movements of the robot, and requires complex
geometric algorithms to properly handle the shape and size of each cell.

-Approximate Decomposition: The space is divided into cells that approximately
cover the free space, and in each cell represents a physical part of the
environment. Compared to the previous case, it is much easier to implement, but
the efficiency is lower.

-Probabilistic Cell Decomposition: Similar to approximate decomposition, but cell
boundaries do not necessarily have a concrete physical meaning. Probabilistic
techniques are used to decide cell divisions, which can be useful in dynamic or
partially known environments, but makes accuracy worse. This is the simplest
cell decomposition method, but may also be the least efficient, as there may be
navigable spaces that have not been taken into account or some free cells may
include non-navigable spaces.

Once the available space has been defined, optimization algorithms can be used to find
the best solution, or at least suboptimal solutions, following the search criteria. The most
commonly used optimizers are A\*, Dijkstra's, Depth-first, breadth-first or variations of any
of these.

$$
\\mathrm {A} ^ {\*}
$$

In general, methods based on cell decomposition are effective for structured
environments where free space and obstacles can be easily segmented. However, it
must be taken into account that the computational cost can be high, that the effectiveness
of this type of methods depends strongly on how the decomposition of the space is
performed and, above all, that do not always guarantee that the solution is globally
optimal.

3.1.2. Classical methods: Potential Fields

Potential field methods are a path planning technique widely used in robotics, especially
for real-time navigation and dynamic environments. This method transforms the space
around the robot into artificial force fields, where the goal generates an attraction and
obstacles a repulsion.

* * *

_**Figure 2. 2-D Potential Field path planning**_

_example. Source: \[22\]._

These fields are mathematically modelled and combined to generate a resultant force field that guides the robot along a path from its initial position to the desired destination. The big problem with these methods is in local minima, where the potential field can guide the robot as it approaches a more positive area, and once inside it cannot leave because that would mean moving into spaces with a more negative potential field.

_**Figure 3. Example of local minima problem. Source: \[22\].**_

To avoid these cases, methodologies have been developed that can help to get out of the local minimum and continue the search of the final goal. The most common one is the Randomized Path Planner, which consists of performing a series of random moves, based on Brownian approximations, then the resulting gradient is calculated after each random move, to check if has been possible to overcome the local minimum \[23\] \[24\].

Other methods would be the Best-First or the Breakout Method. The first is simpler, but less optimal, and consists of making a list of all the possible movement options from the beginning, and start with the path with the highest gradient. If a local minimum is reached, the second-best saved route is taken, and so on until the final goal is reached.

The second method consists on finding a suboptimal path as the first task, without taking into account the constraints that the problem may need to accomplish. Through local changes, the method tries to improve this solution by reducing the number of broken constraints. When the algorithm reaches a local minimum, it increases the ‘weight’ of the violated constraints at that point. This modifies the search ‘landscape’, raising the cost of staying at the local minimum and allowing the algorithm to continue its search \[25\].

Potential field methods are used for several reasons, the simplicity in their implementation means that the computational resources are not high and the search of possible paths is fast, something very useful in dynamic and real-time environments. As mentioned, it is important to take into account their limitations in fields with local minima. However, the combination of these methods with optimizers makes them a very popular option, especially in environments with mobile robots.

## 3.1.3. Classical methods: Road maps

Roadmap methods focus on the construction of a map composed of paths connecting collision-free areas in the robot's workspace. This map acts as a network of possible routes that the robot can follow to reach its destination.

The graph is constructed by taking into account that the nodes represent specific configurations in the space and the edges represent safe paths between these nodes. Once the graph is constructed, a path-finding algorithm is used to find the shortest route from the initial position to the desired final position.

The most commonly used algorithms of this type are:

-Visibility Graphs: Uses the vertices of obstacles and the start and end points to construct the graph, where vertices are directly connected if they are visible to each other without the interference of any obstacle.

_**Figure 4. Visibility Graph example. Source: \[26\].**_

* * *

-Voronoi Diagrams: Creates a graph based on points that are equidistant from the
nearest obstacles, providing paths that maximize the minimum distance to
obstacles, which is useful in tight environments.

Road-mapping methods have become very popular in environments where the map is
known because of its speed in finding viable solutions using samples. As will be
explained in the next section, sample-based methods spread samples across the map
in a random or ordered manner to create the nodes that will be used to create the
roadmap.

3.1.4. Sampling-based algorithms

Sample-based methods are essential used for complex navigation and movement
problems in high-dimensional environments with varying constraints.

As the name suggests, are focused on creating a series of samples across the map,
which will be catalogued and then connected to build the path in a fast and efficient way.
The most prominent algorithms in this field are the Probabilistic Roadmap Method (PRM)
and Rapidly-exploring Random Trees (RRT).

The PRM is a type of roadmap that is separated into two phases: a construction phase
and a query phase. In the first phase, nodes/samples are randomly generated in the free
configuration space, then an attempt is made to connect these nodes by means of simple
segments, avoiding collisions, and finally forming a graph. Given the start and end points,
the second part aims to connect them to the existing graph and then find the shortest
path between them using search and optimization algorithms such as A\* or Dijkstra.

Figure 5. PRM roadmap example. Solid green line
representing the first phase, and dotted line the
second one. Source: \[27\].

It is important to take into account that this method is only used in static environments
where the configuration space does not change, otherwise the second phase would
never be executed. It is especially effective in high-dimensional spaces and can handle
diverse queries efficiently once the graph is constructed. Even the construction process
can be costly, its advantage lies in the reusability of the network for multiple path queries.

* * *

In contrast to PRM, RRT focuses on perform a quick exploration of the configuration space and is particularly useful when a solution needs to be found fast without the need for answers to multiple planning queries.

It starts with an initial node (e.g., the initial configuration of the robot) and is expanded in a tree from this initial point into unexplored regions of the configuration space. At each step, a random point in the space is selected and the tree is extended from the nearest node in the direction of this point, usually with a fixed step length and ensuring that the join does not encounter any obstacles along the line.

In the following figure can be seen that from the initial tree (with black nodes) a new sample is given \[𝑞𝑟𝑎𝑛𝑑\]. Then the nearest node of the tree is found \[𝑞𝑛𝑒𝑎𝑟\] and a line will be created connecting both nodes. Using a predefined step size, the new node is established, and then the collision on the node or in the connection line is checked.

_**Figure 6. Iteration example using RRT algorithm. Source: \[28\].**_

This second method also has variations that seek to improve its qualities. The most widely used is the RRT-Connect, which aims to run the exploration in a bidirectional way,

i.e. making steps from the start and end points, trying to connect both trees using the minimum possible steps \[29\]. Sample-based methods are the most used algorithms in robotics due to their ability to handle efficiently in high-dimensional environments, such as robots with many degrees of freedom.

## 3.1.5. Heuristic-based algorithms

Heuristic methods have provided a different approach to path planning, offering solutions to complex problems using techniques inspired by biological and/or neural processes. Among these, Neural Networks (NN), Genetic Algorithms (GA) and Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) stand out.

Neural Networks are computational models that simulate the way the human brain processes information. They use layers of interconnected nodes or ‘neurons’ to learn, in a first training stage, and then to make predictions about the input data.

* * *

Focusing on path planning, NN allows robots to learn from past plannings and improve
their performance over time. Although they are able to process data from the robot's
sensors, for example, to avoid obstacles. This algorithm is typically used in conjunction
with other planners to find the correct path, especially the first few times.

The neural network is able to learn more quickly to achieve results that are in line with
the global optimum. Then the greatest advantage is the ability to handle complex and
‘noisy’ information, but in return they require large amounts of data for training.

In contrast with neural networks, Genetic Algorithms are inspired by natural selection
laws to perform searches and optimizations. They operate on a ‘population’ of candidate
solutions and apply genetic operators such as selection, crossover and mutation to
evolve better solutions.

Like NNs, GAs are ideal for finding optimal trajectories in complex and dynamic
environments, as they can adjust to changes in the robot's goals. For this reason, they
are often used for mobile robot exploration of large-complex spaces. Like most iterative
methods, the major risk is that they converge to sub-optimal local solutions.

Finally, PSO is an optimization technique based on simulating the social behavior of
swarms, such as birds or fish. It uses a set of individuals or ‘particles’, each representing
a momentary solution, which adjust their position in the solution space based on their
neighbours or their own experience.

Basically, a defined number of particles are initialized in space and randomly scattered
around the robot. Each solution is evaluated against an objective function (which is
wanted to optimize), and the best solution is chosen. If a particle's current position is
better than its best position \[𝑝𝑏𝑒𝑠𝑡\], then its 𝑝𝑏𝑒𝑠𝑡is updated to this new position. If, in
addition, the particle's current position is better than the current global best one \[𝑔𝑏𝑒𝑠𝑡\],
then the 𝑔𝑏𝑒𝑠𝑡is updated.

3.2. Robotic Operating System (ROS)

1
Robot Operating System (ROS) is an open-source middleware for robotic systems that
provides a collection of tools, libraries and conventions that aim to simplify the task of
simulating and controlling a robot.

It was created in 2007 in a collaboration between Standford Artificial Intelligence
Laboratory and Willow Garage. Since then, it has evolved into a standard for use in
industry and academic environments to develop robotic applications, and is now
maintained by Open Robotics.

It works as a modular software framework based on nodes that perform specific tasks
and communicate with each other to transmit information and coordinate to eventually
achieve more complex actions.

In the first version of ROS, nodes were managed through a central node known as the
master, which organizes and facilitates the interaction between the other nodes by
registering names and establishing the necessary connections. It also acted as a
parameter server to centralize configuration and data management, allowing users to
adjust the behaviour of the nodes during execution.

In the second version of ROS (ROS 2, which will be used during this project) this
changes. There is no longer a master node, so each node can connect directly to any
node. This way the data exchange is more direct and allows to create a fully distributed
system. However, it should be noted that, with this change, parameters that could
previously be global, now only exist if the node that creates them is active.

The modular and decentralized design is a great advantage as it makes it extremely
versatile. Added to its existing adaptability to integrate various types of hardware and

Communication between nodes is achieved mainly through two methods: topics and
services. Topics allows the publication and subscription of messages, facilitating the
exchange of information in a continuous and decentralized way. Each message
published in a topic is accessible to all subscribed nodes, allowing for efficient, real-time
communication.

3.2.1. Topics

As introduced above, topics are one of the keystones of communication between the
different nodes in a robotic system. A topic acts as a channel that allows nodes to
exchange messages, making use of publisher and subscriber nodes, which facilitates
decoupled, unidirectional and efficient communication.

Each topic has a message with a content to be transmitted, and where the type of
message must be defined to set the structure of its data. The topic is published by one
or more nodes of the publisher type, and is received by one or more nodes of the
subscriber type, as shown in the following figure:

To illustrate how to work with topics in ROS 2, a simple example has been made where
the first node publishes information about the speed of a robot and the second node
subscribes to this information in order to register it and act accordingly:

Code 1. Node with a publisher called VelocityPublisher that publish strings-type messages.

## 3.2.2. Services

Another way to establish communication between two nodes is through the use of services, which offer bidirectional communication between them, based on a request- response model. As opposed to topics, services are ideal for interacting when an immediate response is required from another node. This makes them suitable for operations that cannot tolerate the asynchronous nature of topics.

Services are defined by two parties: the server and the client. The server provides the service, i.e. it is prepared to process requests and send responses. Clients, on the other hand, are the ones who makes the request for the service and expects to receive a response. As with topics, each service has a message type that defines the structure of the request and the response.

_**Figure 8. ROS server-client communication scheme. Source: \[31\].**_

int rand\_addition = rand() % 10

3.2.4. Unified Robot Description Format (URDF)

URDF (Unified Robot Description Format) files are an XML-based format designed to
describe all aspects of a robot in a structured and detailed way. Because of their clarity
when programming them, they have become the generalized way of modelling robots in
ROS.

URDFs are basically composed of elements that represent
the physical parts of the robot (links) and the connections
between these parts (joints). The general components are:

-: The root element that encapsulates all other
elements. It must have a name attribute that defines
the name of the robot.
-: Defines a link or rigid part of the robot. Each

-: Defines a link or rigid part of the robot. Each
link has its own visual identity, mass, and inertial
properties.
-: Defines a joint between two links, specifying

Figure 9. 3-DoF manipulator
scheme. Source: \[22\]

-: Defines a joint between two links, specifying
how they can move relative to each other.

* * *

-: Describes what the link looks like. May include details about the shape,
size, and material.

-: Defines the geometry used for collision detection, which may be
different from the visual geometry to simplify calculations, and may not even be
used if collisions are not to be taken into account.

Finally, the main components for the definition of a joint are:

- and : Identify the links that the joint connects, where the parent
is the link closest to the base of the robot and the child is the next link in the chain
that is connected by the joint being defined.

-: Defines the position and orientation of the joint's reference system
relative to the parent link. It is optional.

-: Specifies the axis around which the joint allows movement (optional).

-: Sets limits on movement, such as maximum and minimum angle or
maximum velocity and force.

-: Defines dynamic properties such as damping and friction.

-: Sets safety parameters that limit the movement of the joint
under certain conditions, also optional.

# 4\. MoveIt 2

MoveIt is a path planning and robot manipulation tool widely used in the robotics community, developed as part of the ROS middleware.

This package provides a set of functionalities for path planning, collision avoidance, inverse kinematics and control of robotic manipulators. MoveIt's main strength lies in its ability to simplify the complexity of robot motion programming, and in the capacity to integrate a variety of sensor and controller systems.

One of MoveIt's most prominent components is the MoveIt Setup Assistant, an intuitive tool that makes it easy to import and configure robotic models.

## 4.1. Integration of a robot model

Through a moderately guided process, the Setup Assistant allows the definition of essential robot parameters, such as joints, motion limits, and collision surfaces. This not only speeds up and simplifies the configuration process but also significantly reduces the margin of error, allowing developers to focus on trajectory planning and execution rather than configuration details.

### 4.1.1. From SolidWorks to URDF

The first step would be to obtain the robot's URDF (see chapter 3.2.4), as it is the simplest way to use MoveIt's Setup Assistant. For this, if the user does not have sufficient knowledge to create the URDF from scratch, or simply does not wish to, some tools provided by other applications can be used. The most common case is using the SolidWorks to URDF exporter.

This exporter is a free package offered by the SolidWorks application that, through axes and coordinate systems, can create the URDF file with the same characteristics as in SolidWorks.

Starting from the 3D model of the robot, the rotation axes of the manipulator's joints and the reference axes of the links must be defined. In the case of the UR3 robot (the model that will be used for this project) from Universal Robots3, 6 reference axes and 7 rotation axes are needed to correctly define the joints and achieve the 6 degrees of freedom it has. In the case of the gripper, only 2 are needed, as an angular gripper will be used. Figures 10 and 11 show the axes of the robot and the gripper, respectively.

# 4.2. Launching a robot with MoveIt

With the robot model already integrated into the system, it is time to launch it to visualize it in RViz and interact with MoveIt's GUI. However, several nodes need to be launched for everything to work properly and to use all the functionalities the program offers.

For this, it is necessary to understand what MoveIt requires and how its nodes communicate with each other, with RViz, and with ROS.

## 4.2.1. Move group node

The move\_group node is the central component of MoveIt, acting as an integrator of all the necessary subsystems for motion planning and execution. This node provides ROS services and actions that allow users to interact with the robot for manipulation and navigation tasks.

* * *

It mainly handles grouping several essential components such as motion planning, collision detection, and trajectory execution. Users can interact with it through C++ code (using the move\_group\_interface node), Python (with the MoveIt\_commander node), or via the GUI using the motion planning plugin in RViz.

_**Figure 17. General scheme of the connection between MoveIt nodes. Source: \[35\].**_

Once user requests are received, the move\_group node communicates with the rest of the nodes to execute the necessary actions. Among the most important nodes are:

-Planning Scene: Maintains a representation of the environment and the robot's state, including objects that may be attached to it. This is managed through the Planning Scene Monitor. -Planning Pipeline: Provides the infrastructure for motion planning. It first reads the current state of the robot through the Ajoint\_states topic of ROS and communicates with the Planning Interface sub-node.

* * *

-Planning Interface: Uses external planner packages to create paths. -Collision Detection: Ensures that the trajectories do not result in collisions. -Trajectory Execution Manager: Responsible for executing the planned trajectories using the robot controllers created earlier.

However, although all these nodes are crucial for the robot's movement, they need to communicate with the robot\_description parameter to understand its properties. These properties are defined in the URDF and the Semantic Robot Description Format (SRDF). The URDF, as mentioned earlier, describes the robot's physical model, while the SRDF specifies the joint groups (arm and hand), predefined configurations of the robot (initial, hand open, and hand close), additional collision checking information (collision matrix), and properties such as the end effector.

With this understanding, the configuration and launch of the main node can be defined as follows:

_**Code 9. Configuration of the move\_group node.**_ MoveIt\_config = ( MoveItConfigsBuilder(robot\_name="ur3\_robot", package\_name="ur3\_def") .robot\_description(file\_path="config/ur3\_robot.urdf.xacro") .robot\_description\_semantic(file\_path="config/ur3\_robot.srdf") .planning\_scene\_monitor(publish\_robot\_description=True, publish\_robot\_description\_semantic=True) .trajectory\_execution(file\_path="config/MoveIt\_controllers.yaml") .planning\_pipelines(pipelines=\["ompl", "chomp", "pilz\_industrial\_motion\_planner", "stomp"\]) .to\_MoveIt\_configs() )

_**Code 10. Creation and initialization of the move\_group node.**_ move\_group\_node = Node( package="MoveIt\_ros\_move\_group", executable="move\_group", output="screen", parameters=\[MoveIt\_config.to\_dict()\], arguments=\["--ros-args", "--log-level", "info"\], )

## 4.2.2. Other nodes

As mentioned earlier, the move\_group is not the only node that needs to be launched, as there are other essential nodes for the proper functioning of the system. The most important ones are:

-RViz node: Launches RViz with the desired configuration to visualize the robot and its planning environments.

* * *

-Static tf node: Publishes a static transformation between the world and base\_link reference frames. This is necessary to provide a reference for the robot's position in the global space. -Robot State Publisher: Publishes the robot's state, such as the position of the joints and the end effector, to the ROS nodes. As a node, it allows the rest of the system's nodes to know the robot's current configuration at any time. -Joint State Broadcaster: Reads all state interfaces and publishes them on the /joint\_states topic. It is used to send the state of specific joints between nodes. -ROS 2 Control Node: Runs the ROS 2 controller manager. It essentially uses the controllers created with the Setup Assistant to control the robot in real time. Recall that MoveIt's controllers are used to create trajectories, but ROS controllers are necessary to execute them. -UR3 Arm/Hand Controller Spawner: These two nodes are used to launch the spawners for the arm and hand controllers of the robot, which are governed by the YAML files created in the Config folder. -TF Node: This is not launched directly in this code because it is a tool integrated into ROS, but it is used during the application’s operation. It monitors transformation information using the TF (Transform) library of ROS, which calculates the transformations between joints to obtain the robot's global pose at all times. Without this node, the other nodes could not calculate the robot's pose either.

Once all these nodes are created in a launch file (see section 4 of the annexes), it should be compiled and then executed.

## 4.4.1. Collision Objects

Creating a collision object can be done in two ways. The first, as previously mentioned, can be done directly from the application's graphical interface. In the Scene Objects section, objects of different shapes can be created, and their size and position can be chosen.

The second way is through scripts, which is necessary to create predefined environments and execute them at the beginning of simulations. Below is the code needed to create a box-shaped obstacle:

_**Code 14. Script for create a box obstacle and visualize it in MoveIt.**_

**auto** const collision\_object = \[frame\_id = move\_group\_interface.getPlanningFrame()\]{ MoveIt\_msgs::msg::CollisionObject collision\_object; collision\_object.header.frame\_id = frame\_id; collision\_object.id = "box1"; shape\_msgs::msg::SolidPrimitive primitive;

_// Define the size of the box in meters_ primitive.type = primitive.BOX; primitive.dimensions.resize(3); primitive.dimensions\[primitive.BOX\_X\] = 0.5; primitive.dimensions\[primitive.BOX\_Y\] = 0.1; primitive.dimensions\[primitive.BOX\_Z\] = 0.5;

_// Define the pose of the box (relative to the frame\_id)_ geometry\_msgs::msg::Pose box\_pose; box\_pose.orientation.w = 1.0; box\_pose.position.x = 0.2; box\_pose.position.y = 0.2; box\_pose.position.z = 0.25;

collision\_object.primitives.push\_back(primitive); collision\_object.primitive\_poses.push\_back(box\_pose); collision\_object.operation = collision\_object.ADD;

**return collision\_object;** };

* * *

_// Add the collision object to the scene_ MoveIt::planning\_interface::PlanningSceneInterface planning\_scene\_interface; planning\_scene\_interface.applyCollisionObject(collision\_object);

Initially, a function is defined that captures the _frame\_id_ of the move group (move\_group\_interface.getPlanningFrame()). Within this function, a collision\_object of type MoveIt\_msgs::msg::CollisionObject is declared, setting its header with the captured _frame\_id and assigning it a unique identifier, in this case "box1"._

Next, a primitive object is defined to represent a box. It is specified that the type of the primitive is a box and its dimensions are added in meters. This sets the size of the collision object in the three-dimensional space.

Then, the pose of the box is defined using a _box\_pose_ object of type _geometry\_msgs::msg::Pose. The orientation of the box is set with a quaternion where_ only the w component is 1.0, indicating a neutral orientation. The position of the box is defined relative to the frame\_id, with coordinates x = 0.2, y = 0.2, and z = 0.25.

To finish the function, the primitive object is added to the vector of primitives of the _collision\_object, and the pose of the box is added to the vector of primitive poses of the_ same object. The operation of the collision object is set to ADD so that it is added to the robot's planning environment. The lambda function returns the configured _collision\_object._

With this collision object, it only needs to be added to the planning frame of the MoveIt environment that was captured at the beginning, giving it the characteristic of a collision object, so that it is considered when generating trajectories.

# 4.5. Pick and place

For performing multi-stage movements, such as the typical pick-and-place operation, the previous steps need to be replicated with certain differences. It is necessary to add rectilinear movements in the stages of approaching objects, allow collision between the gripper and the object when picking it up, and link them together to avoid dependency on simulation dynamics.

Since programming all this can be very repetitive and tedious, the designers of MoveIt have created the MoveIt Task Constructor (MTC). MTC is a powerful tool for planning complex operations that consist of multiple subtasks, known as stages.

This approach allows decomposing complex motion planning problems into a series of more manageable subproblems. The idea is to learn how to define these complex movements as a series of simpler movements to better organize, design, and reuse the code.

* * *

## 4.5.1. Primitive stages

Simple or primitive stages are the basic isocks that define specific actions in task planning. Each stage represents a step in the planning process, such as making a point- to-point movement, opening a gripper, or calculating an inverse kinematic solution.

The stages are categorized into three types:

- **Generators (↕): These stages calculate their results independently, generating** them from scratch, and propagate these results both forward and backward in the planning sequence. An example would be the inverse kinematics (IK) solver that generates geometric poses. Approach and retreat movements depend on the generated solution. - **Propagators (⇕ / ↑ / ↓): These stages receive an input state from a neighbouring** stage, solve a subproblem, and then propagate the solution state to the neighbouring stage in the opposite direction. They can act in both directions, from start to end or vice versa. An example would be calculating a Cartesian trajectory based on an initial state or a goal state, such as an approach movement. - **Connectors (∥):** As the name suggests, these stages attempt to connect resulting states from both neighbouring stages without propagating results themselves, aiming to generate a continuous trajectory. An example could be the free movement of the robot from one state to another.

## 4.5.2. Containers stages

Container stages are a special type of stage that can contain other stages. Their main function is to organize and structure the sequence of stages into a more complex task.

## Containers are differentiated into:

-Wrappers: Encapsulate a single subordinate stage and modify or filter the results of that stage. An example would be a result filtering stage that only accepts solutions from its child stage that meet a certain criterion, or an IK wrapper stage that generates inverse kinematic solutions based on planning scenes. -Serial Containers: Maintain a sequence of subordinate stages that must be executed in a specific order to achieve the complete task. Their function is to combine multiple sequential stages, considering only end-to-end solutions as results. An example would be a pick movement that consists of a sequence of steps such as approach, grasp, lift, and retreat. -Parallel Containers: Combine sets of subordinate stages that can be executed in parallel, selecting the best solution among them. This solution can be very useful for planning alternative solutions to the main one, for example, in a free movement.

* * *

4.5.3. Example of pick and place tasks

Next, the process of organizing stages to plan a pick-and-place task will be explained.

The process begins with an initial stage called "current state." This generator stage
represents the current state of the robot and the environment, capturing the initial state
of the robot and the planning scene. It essentially collects the information that will serve
as the starting point for the planning.

( ) current state

The next stage is a connector that plans a free movement from the robot's current
position to an approach position near the object. This stage is crucial for positioning the
robot's end effector in a suitable place to start the grasp sequence.

( ) current state
(II) move to pick

To start the Pick subsequence, a container called "pick the object" will be needed, which
will group all the necessary stages to pick up the object in a logical sequence. Within this
container, the "approach" stage moves the robot's end effector toward the pre-grasp
position, using a propagator to approach the object in a controlled manner. Next, there
is a Wrapper stage that analyzes the results of its contained stage “generate place pose,”
which generates multiple possible poses for grasping the object and selects a viable
pose through inverse kinematics calculations.

Once the grasp pose has been determined, a stage "allow contact" should be created to
modify the planning scene, allowing collisions between the end effector and the object
to ensure the grasp can be performed without restrictions. The next stage, "close
gripper," moves the robot's gripper to the closed position to secure the object, while
"attach object" updates the planning scene to reflect that the object is now attached to
the robot. Finally, the "lift object" stage lifts the object from its original place, completing
the pick action.

* * *

After picking up the object, another stage identical to "move to place" should be created to connect the robot's current position to the desired placement position via a free movement, adding the approach distance for placing.

The procedure for the second part of the task is very similar to the pick, creating a container called "place object" that organizes all the necessary stages to place the object in the desired location. The obvious difference is that instead of closing the end effector, upon reaching the place position, it will open, and instead of linking it with an attach, it will unlink.

To finish, a "move to home" stage is usually created, which plans a movement to return the robot to its initial position or a safe resting position. This stage closes the loop of the "pick and place" task, ensuring that the robot is ready for the next operation or a safe starting configuration.
